scenario: A B2B procurement workflow involves two separate companies -- Vendor and Buyer. Each has their own internal process and communicates with the other only via messages (purchase orders, invoices, shipment notifications). The model must clearly show inter-organisational boundaries.
question: Which BPMN construct fits?

options.text: Single Pool with two Lanes (Vendor and Buyer). In this model, Lanes subdivide responsibilities within a single Pool, representing different roles or departments inside one organisation. Lanes do not model separate organisational participants; they share the same process definition, variable scope, and deployment unit. Since Vendor and Buyer are distinct external organisations, a single-pool Lane model fails to capture the contractual boundary between participants.

Two Pools -- one per organisation -- connected by Message Flows. Each Pool represents a separate BPMN participant with its own independent process definition, lifecycle, variable scope, and deployment unit. Communication between Pools crosses the organisational boundary exclusively via Message Flows (dashed arrows), making the inter-company contract explicit in the model. 0 Collaboration pattern -- mandatory for modelling separate legal entities like Vendor and Buyer exchanging documents.

Two Call Activities calling each other reciprocally. Call Activities invoke a child process definition from a parent process; they are used for hierarchical reuse within one organisation, not for modelling two autonomous organisations that communicate via message exchange. A bidirectional Call Activity setup would create problematic circular invocation dependencies. Call Activities are designed for hierarchical parent-child reuse within a single organisation, not for peer-to-peer inter-organisational communication across separate process boundaries.

Single Pool with annotation text explaining who does what. Text annotations in BPMN are documentation artefacts that add human-readable notes to a diagram element. They carry no execution semantics, do not establish separate process scopes, and are ignored by the Zeebe engine at runtime. Using only annotations to separate organisations loses all structural modelling benefits and produces a process diagram with no structural enforcement.

a.text: Incorrect. Multiple Pools represent separate participants/organisations. Each Pool is independently deployable. Inter-Pool communication uses Message Flows (dashed connector) -- exactly the inter-org case described.

b.text: Correct. Lanes are for internal teams within one organisation/process, not separate orgs.

c.text: Incorrect. Two-way Call Activities create circular dependencies; Message Flows are the canonical inter-org pattern.

d.text: Incorrect. Annotation text isn't machine-readable; defeats the inter-org modelling.

explanation: Lanes are for internal teams within one organisation/process, not separate orgs.
